title: Critical Reasoning: Find the Flaw
prerequisites: identify an argument's conclusion and evidence
learning_objectives: Name the specific reasoning error an argument commits.
Recognize classic flaws: ad hominem, appeal to popularity, post hoc, hasty generalization, appeal to ignorance, slippery slope.
Match the abstract flaw description to what the argument actually did.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

opening.builds_on: Separating an argument's conclusion from its evidence.

items.prompt: Name the flaw: 'X happened after Y, so Y caused X.'
Name the flaw: 'Most people buy it, so it's the best.'
Name the flaw: 'No one has proven it's harmful, so it's safe.'

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls the named flaw families so learners can match them to the argument.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Have them describe in their own words what the author did wrong before reading the choices.

questions.prompt: Name the flaw: 'She's rich, so ignore her economic argument.'
Name the flaw: 'Every swan I've seen is white, so all swans are white.'
questions.answer: Hasty generalization from a limited sample.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You can find a conclusion and its evidence. A flaw question asks WHY the leap between them is illegitimate - and the answer is usually one of a handful of named reasoning errors. Describe the mistake in your own words first, then match it.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can name the specific reasoning error an argument commits and match it to the correct abstract description.
success_criteria: I can describe, in plain words, what the argument did wrong.
I can recognize the classic named flaws.
I can match my description to the answer choice that states it.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem: Critic: The restaurant's owner says his food is the healthiest in town, but he is trying to sell meals, so his claim is worthless. The critic's reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

options.A: rejects a claim based on the source's motive rather than on any evidence about the claim itself.
options.B: relies on a sample of diners that is too small.
options.C: assumes that healthy food cannot be profitable.
options.D: confuses a cause with an effect.
options.E: treats the owner's opinion as expert testimony.

i_do.explanation: The critic dismisses the health claim only because the owner has a motive to sell - an ad hominem that ignores whether the food is actually healthy. A names that error.
think_aloud_steps: Conclusion: the owner's claim is worthless. Evidence: he wants to sell meals.
In my words: attacking the person's motive, not the claim - that's ad hominem.
Find the choice describing that: A.
The other choices name flaws the argument didn't commit.
i_do.key_takeaway: Describe the reasoning error in plain words first, then match it to the choice - don't be lured by real flaws the argument didn't make.

we_do.stem: Advertisement: More dentists choose BrightSmile toothpaste than any other brand, so BrightSmile must be the most effective toothpaste. The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

options.A: treats a product's popularity as proof of its effectiveness.
options.B: relies on the opinion of a single dentist.
options.D: ignores the price of competing toothpastes.

we_do.explanation: The ad slides from 'most chosen' to 'most effective' - an appeal to popularity. A names that error.
scaffold_hints: What is the evidence, and what is concluded from it?
Popularity -> quality is a classic named flaw.
Match your description to the choice.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: The ad cites many dentists, not one; the core error is popularity -> quality.

we_do.stem: Politician: No study has ever shown that our new tax policy harms small businesses, so the policy is clearly safe for them. The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

options.A: treats the absence of evidence of harm as proof that there is no harm.
options.B: relies on studies funded by the government.
options.C: assumes all small businesses are alike.
options.E: attacks the motives of the policy's critics.

we_do.explanation: Concluding the policy is safe merely because harm hasn't been shown is an appeal to ignorance. A names it.
scaffold_hints: The evidence is a LACK of evidence.
Absence of proof of harm is not proof of safety.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: The argument doesn't attack critics or cite studies; it leans on missing evidence.

you_do.stem: Manager: Right after we repainted the office, output went up, so the new paint color is boosting productivity. The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

options.A: concludes that the paint caused the rise simply because the rise came after the painting.
options.B: relies on the testimony of a few employees.
options.C: assumes all employees like the color.
options.D: ignores the cost of the paint.
options.E: treats productivity and morale as the same thing.

you_do.explanation: Inferring causation from mere sequence (output rose after painting) is a post hoc flaw. A names it.

you_do.stem: Editorial: If the city allows food trucks on Main Street, soon every sidewalk will be blocked and no pedestrian will be able to walk downtown. So food trucks must be banned. The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

options.A: assumes without support that a limited step will inevitably lead to an extreme outcome.
options.C: attacks the character of food-truck owners.
options.D: confuses a necessary condition with a sufficient one.
options.E: treats popularity as evidence of quality.

you_do.explanation: Claiming one modest allowance must snowball into total blockage is a slippery-slope flaw. A names it.

you_do.stem: Reviewer: This author's last three novels were bestsellers, so her next book is certain to be a bestseller too. The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

options.A: assumes that a past pattern must continue without considering factors that could change the outcome.
options.B: relies on the opinions of unqualified readers.
options.C: attacks the author rather than the books.
options.E: treats the absence of failure as proof of success.

you_do.explanation: Projecting a past streak forward as a certainty ignores factors that could break the pattern - an unwarranted assumption of continuity. A names it.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
